source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
title: Ensure Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted using keys managed in Cloud KMS

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
input_row.title: Ensure Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted using keys managed in Cloud KMS
input_row.description: Encrypt Kubernetes secrets, stored in etcd, at the application-layer using a customer- managed key in Cloud KMS.
input_row.rationale: By default, GKE encrypts customer content stored at rest, including Secrets. GKE handles and manages this default encryption for you without any additional action on your part. Application-layer Secrets Encryption provides an additional layer of security for sensitive data, such as user defined Secrets and Secrets required for the operation of the cluster, such as service account keys, which are all stored in etcd. Using this functionality, you can use a key, that you manage in Cloud KMS, to encrypt data at the application layer. This protects against attackers in the event that they manage to gain access to etcd. Impact: To use the Cloud KMS CryptoKey to protect etcd in the cluster, the 'Kubernetes Engine Service Agent' Service account must hold the 'Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter' role.
input_row.audit: Using Google Cloud Console: 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list 2. From the list of clusters, click on each cluster to bring up the Details pane, and ensure Application-layer Secrets Encryption is set to 'Enabled'. Using Command Line: gcloud container clusters describe $CLUSTER_NAME --zone $COMPUTE_ZONE -- format json | jq '.databaseEncryption' If configured correctly, the output from the command returns a response containing the following detail: keyName=projects/<key_project_id>/locations/<location>/keyRings/<ring_name>/c ryptoKeys/<key_name>] state=ENCRYPTED { "currentState": "CURRENT_STATE_ENCRYPTED", "keyName": "projects/<key_project_id>/locations/us- central1/keyRings/<ring_name>/cryptoKeys/<key_name>", "state": "ENCRYPTED" }
input_row.remediation: To enable Application-layer Secrets Encryption, several configuration items are required. These include: • A key ring • A key • A GKE service account with Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter role Once these are created, Application-layer Secrets Encryption can be enabled on an existing or new cluster. Using Google Cloud Console: To create a key 1. Go to Cloud KMS by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/security/kms. 2. Select CREATE KEY RING. 3. Enter a Key ring name and the region where the keys will be stored. 4. Click CREATE. 5. Enter a Key name and appropriate rotation period within the Create key pane. 6. Click CREATE. To enable on a new cluster 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. Click CREATE CLUSTER, and choose the required cluster mode. 3. Within the Security heading, under CLUSTER, check Encrypt secrets at the application layer checkbox. 4. Select the kms key as the customer-managed key and, if prompted, grant permissions to the GKE Service account. 5. Click CREATE. To enable on an existing cluster 1. Go to Kubernetes Engine by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/kubernetes/list. 2. Select the cluster to be updated. 3. Under the Details pane, within the Security heading, click on the pencil named Application-layer secrets encryption. 4. Enable Encrypt secrets at the application layer and choose a kms key. 5. Click SAVE CHANGES. Using Command Line: To create a key: Create a key ring: gcloud kms keyrings create <ring_name> --location <location> --project <key_project_id> Create a key: gcloud kms keys create <key_name> --location <location> --keyring <ring_name> --purpose encryption --project <key_project_id> Grant the Kubernetes Engine Service Agent service account the Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter role: gcloud kms keys add-iam-policy-binding <key_name> --location <location> -- keyring <ring_name> --member serviceAccount:<service_account_name> --role roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter --project <key_project_id> To create a new cluster with Application-layer Secrets Encryption: gcloud container clusters create <cluster_name> --cluster-version=latest -- zone <zone> --database-encryption-key projects/<key_project_id>/locations/<location>/keyRings/<ring_name>/cryptoKey s/<key_name> --project <cluster_project_id> To enable on an existing cluster: gcloud container clusters update <cluster_name> --zone <zone> --database- encryption-key projects/<key_project_id>/locations/<location>/keyRings/<ring_name>/cryptoKey s/<key_name> --project <cluster_project_id> Default Value: By default, Application-layer Secrets Encryption is disabled. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/encrypting-secrets
input_row.impact: To use the Cloud KMS CryptoKey to protect etcd in the cluster, the 'Kubernetes Engine Service Agent' Service account must hold the 'Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter' role.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified via GCP APIs, Google Cloud SDK (gcloud), and GCP CLI. The audit field provides clear gcloud command examples that allow the user to verify whether the Kubernetes Secrets are encrypted using keys managed in Cloud KMS. This includes checking the `databaseEncryption` state and the associated `keyName` settings. 

This control involves standard GCP service properties related to encryption using Cloud KMS, which are accessible via the GCP API. Therefore, it meets the criteria for an automated approach due to the following reasons:
- The capability to query specific properties using GCP APIs.
- It involves GCP's encryption management, a standard feature of GCP services.
- The Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) and CLI commands to confirm the configuration are explicitly detailed.
- The aspects under audit (encryption using Cloud KMS) are easily checkable through GCP APIs.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_gke_cluster_application_layer_secrets_encryption_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- Use the Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) command `gcloud container clusters describe` to fetch the encryption settings of the cluster.
- Specifically, query the `.databaseEncryption` field to ensure:
  - The `state` is set to `ENCRYPTED`.
  - The `keyName` follows the correct structure: `projects/<key_project_id>/locations/<location>/keyRings/<ring_name>/cryptoKeys/<key_name>`.
- Enumerate all GKE clusters within the project to confirm each cluster individually for compliance.
- Pass Criteria: 
  - For each GKE cluster, `databaseEncryption.state` should be `ENCRYPTED`, and `keyName` should be correctly formatted as specified.
- Ensure that the 'Kubernetes Engine Service Agent' service account holds the 'Cloud KMS CryptoKey Encrypter/Decrypter' role on the KMS CryptoKey used.

By automating this check, CSPM tools can ensure continuous compliance monitoring for Kubernetes Secrets encryption using customer-managed keys in Cloud KMS.
step2_review:
---

### 1. **APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- **Manual vs Automated:** The classification as "Automated" is correct. All required checks (encryption state, key association, service account permissions) can be performed via GCP APIs and Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`), without requiring manual inspection.
- **GCP SDK/CLI Coverage:** The `gcloud container clusters describe` command exposes the `databaseEncryption` field, which includes both `state` and `keyName`. Enumerating clusters and checking these fields is fully automatable.
- **Other GCP Tools:** GCP Config and CSPM tools (like Forseti or Security Command Center) can also automate this check via API calls. CloudFormation is AWS-specific and not relevant here.

---

### 2. **TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8/10

- **Automation Details:** The use of `gcloud container clusters describe` is correct for retrieving encryption settings. Enumerating clusters and checking `databaseEncryption.state` and `keyName` is technically accurate.
- **Service Account Permissions:** The requirement to check that the "Kubernetes Engine Service Agent" has the `roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter` role is correct and necessary for encryption/decryption operations.
- **Program Name:** The program name (`gcp_gke_cluster_application_layer_secrets_encryption_enabled`) is descriptive but could be more explicit (see Improvements).
- **Minor Gaps:**
    - The assessment does not specify the exact gcloud command for checking the service account permissions on the KMS key, which is a critical part of the control.
    - It does not mention checking for clusters where `databaseEncryption.state` is `DECRYPTED` or missing, which should be flagged as non-compliant.
    - No mention of handling regional vs zonal clusters, which may affect enumeration.
    - No explicit reference to the GCP REST API (`projects.locations.clusters.get`) as an alternative to `gcloud`.

---

### 3. **IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Service Account Permissions Check:**  
  Add explicit instructions for checking that the GKE Service Agent (typically `service-<project-number>@container-engine-robot.iam.gserviceaccount.com`) has the `roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter` role on the specified KMS key.  
  Example command:
  ```bash
  gcloud kms keys get-iam-policy <key_name> --keyring=<ring_name> --location=<location> --project=<key_project_id>
  ```
  Then, parse for the GKE Service Agent and the required role.

- **Cluster Enumeration:**  
  Specify the use of:
  ```bash
  gcloud container clusters list --format="value(name,location)"
  ```
  and iterate over all clusters (regional and zonal).

- **Non-Compliant States:**  
  Explicitly state that clusters with `databaseEncryption.state` not set to `ENCRYPTED` (i.e., `DECRYPTED` or missing) are non-compliant.

- **API Alternative:**  
  Note that the same checks can be performed via the GCP REST API (`projects.locations.clusters.get`).

- **Program Name Suggestion:**  
  For clarity and convention, consider:  
  `gcp_gke_cluster_secrets_at_rest_cmek_encryption_enabled`  
  This makes it explicit that the check is for "secrets at rest" and "customer-managed encryption keys (CMEK)".

---

### 4. **CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is technically sound, and the control can be fully automated using GCP APIs and SDKs. Minor improvements are suggested for completeness and clarity.

---

### 5. **FINAL RECOMMENDATION:** MODIFY

- **Keep the automated classification.**
- **Modify** the automation details to:
    - Explicitly include checking the GKE Service Agent's permissions on the KMS key.
    - Specify enumeration of both regional and zonal clusters.
    - Clearly state the handling of non-compliant states.
    - Suggest a more precise program name as per above.
    - Optionally, reference the GCP REST API as an alternative to `gcloud`.

---

**Summary Table:**

| Section               | Result/Score | Notes                                                                                           |
|-----------------------|--------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Approach Validation   | AGREE        | Fully automatable via GCP SDK/API/CLI                                                           |
| Technical Accuracy    | 8/10         | Correct, but missing explicit service account permission check and some enumeration details      |
| Improvements          | N/A          | Add permission check, clarify enumeration, improve program name, handle non-compliant states     |
| Confidence            | HIGH         | Minor gaps, but fundamentally sound                                                             |
| Final Recommendation  | MODIFY       | Update automation details and program name for clarity and completeness                          |

---

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that all required audit checks—verifying application-layer secrets encryption and the use of customer-managed Cloud KMS keys—are fully exposed via GCP APIs and the Google Cloud SDK (`gcloud`). The `databaseEncryption` field, including `state` and `keyName`, is directly accessible for every GKE cluster. Additionally, service account permissions can be programmatically validated. No manual inspection is required, and all compliance criteria can be checked reliably and repeatably via automation.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_gke_cluster_application_layer_secrets_encryption_kms_cmek_enabled

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- **GCP Resource/Service:** Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Clusters
- **API/CLI Method:**  
  - List clusters: `gcloud container clusters list --format="value(name,location)"`
  - Describe cluster: `gcloud container clusters describe <CLUSTER_NAME> --region=<REGION> --format=json`
  - Check service account permissions: `gcloud projects get-iam-policy <PROJECT_ID> --flatten="bindings[].members" --filter="bindings.members:<serviceAccount>" --format="table(bindings.role)"`
- **Properties to Check:**  
  - `.databaseEncryption.state` (should be `ENCRYPTED`)
  - `.databaseEncryption.keyName` (should match `projects/<key_project_id>/locations/<location>/keyRings/<ring_name>/cryptoKeys/<key_name>`)
  - The GKE service account (Kubernetes Engine Service Agent) has `roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter` on the specified KMS key.
- **Pass Criteria:**  
  - For every GKE cluster:  
    - `databaseEncryption.state` is `ENCRYPTED`
    - `databaseEncryption.keyName` is present and references a valid KMS key
    - The GKE service account has the `roles/cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter` role on the referenced key
- **Fail Criteria:**  
  - Any cluster where:
    - `databaseEncryption.state` is not `ENCRYPTED`
    - `databaseEncryption.keyName` is missing or does not reference a valid KMS key
    - The required service account role is missing

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
There were no substantive disagreements; both AI opinions supported automation. The second opinion suggested a slightly more descriptive program name and highlighted the importance of checking service account permissions, which is included in the final automation details for completeness and accuracy.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE KUBERNETES ENGINE (GKE) BENCHMARK V1.7.0 PDF
